content.problemStatement:
JJ gives you a number NNN and challenges you to construct a permutation PPP of length NNN such that:


∣Pi+1−Pi∣≥2|P_{i + 1} - P_i| \ge 2∣Pi+1​−Pi​∣≥2 for all 1≤i≤N−11 \le i \le N - 11≤i≤N−1 (Here ∣x∣|x|∣x∣ denotes the absolute value of xxx)
Can you complete JJ's challenge?

As a reminder, a permutation of length NNN is an array where every integer from 111 to NNN occurs exactly once.


Input Format

The first line contains a single integer TTT — the number of test cases. Then the test cases follow.
The first and only line of each test case contains one integer NNN — the length of the permutation PPP to be constructed.


Output Format
For each test case, output NNN space-separated integers P1,P2,…,PNP_1, P_2, \ldots, P_NP1​,P2​,…,PN​ denoting the permutation PPP which satisfies the given conditions.


If no such permutation exists, output −1-1−1.


If there are multiple answers, print any.
constraints: Sum of N over all test cases ≤ 2·10^5
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. Each of the next T lines contains a single integer N (2 ≤ N ≤ 10^5).
content.outputFormat: For each test case, output a permutation of length N satisfying |P_{i+1}-P_i| ≥ 2 for all 1 ≤ i < N. Print the N numbers separated by spaces on one line. If no such permutation exists, output -1.

sampleTestCases.explanation: For N=2 no permutation satisfies the condition, so -1 is printed. For N=6 the construction odds then evens gives 1 3 5 2 4 6, which meets |P_{i+1}-P_i| ≥ 2.
N=4 is a special case; the permutation 2 4 1 3 has adjacent differences 2,3,2, all ≥2.

solution.approach:
The condition forbids two consecutive numbers from being adjacent in the permutation. For N = 2 or N = 3 it is impossible, because every permutation contains at least one pair of consecutive integers. For N = 4 a concrete valid permutation is 2 4 1 3. For all N ≥ 5 we can place all odd numbers in increasing order followed by all even numbers in increasing order. Between the last odd (which is N if N is odd, otherwise N‑1) and the first even (2) the difference is at least 3, and any two consecutive odds or evens differ by exactly 2, satisfying the requirement. The algorithm therefore is:
1. If N == 2 or N == 3 → print -1.
2. Else if N == 4 → print "2 4 1 3".
3. Else → print odds 1,3,5,… then evens 2,4,6,….
The construction is O(N) per test case and uses O(1) extra space besides the output.
solution.timeComplexity: O(N) per test case, total O(sum of N) ≤ 2·10^5.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(1) auxiliary space (output array not counted).
